mnemonics.title: SCHEDULING CRITERIA
mnemonics.content: CUPS TWR - CPU Utilization, Throughput, Response Time, Turnaround time, Waiting time, Response time (remember both T and R)

mnemonics.title: SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS TYPES
mnemonics.content: PREEMPTIVE - P for Priority, R for Round Robin (time slices), E for Earliest Deadline First, E for Multilevel Feedback, M for Multiprocessor (all use preemption)

mnemonics.title: FCFS PROBLEMS
mnemonics.content: FCFS = First Come First Served = Fails Causing Very Slow processing (Convoy effect)

acronyms.usage: Algorithm that picks shortest burst time thread first to minimize average waiting time
Simple scheduling algorithm that serves requests in order of arrival
Time-sliced scheduling where each thread gets equal CPU time cyclically
Defines competition boundaries for threads (process-level or system-level)
Threads within same process compete for CPU
All threads in system compete for CPU
Multiple CPUs share memory and schedule from common ready queue
Real-time scheduling based on task frequency

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a carousel or merry-go-round where each thread gets one ride (quantum) before moving to the back of the line. The carousel keeps spinning indefinitely.
Think of a hospital emergency room - critical patients (high priority) are seen immediately, while others wait in priority order.
Imagine multiple checkout lines at a store - express lane for quick items, regular lanes for normal shopping, and separate lanes for bulk items - each with its own rules.
Think of each CPU as a desk with personal items. Once you work at your desk, your things stay there. Moving to another desk means starting fresh - that's why binding helps cache.
Imagine one slow truck leading a line of fast cars on a highway - everyone is stuck behind the slow vehicle despite being faster.

analogies.analogy: Thread scheduling is like a teacher managing classroom activities - deciding which student (thread) speaks when, how long each can speak, and who gets priority for questions.
Time quantum is like the timer in a debate competition - each speaker gets 2 minutes, when time is up they're interrupted and someone else speaks.
Context switching is like a professor marking exam papers - they save their progress (stack), pick up another stack of papers (registers), and start fresh on the new task.
Starvation is like a neglected plant in a classroom - other plants get all the water and sunlight (CPU time) while one keeps waiting indefinitely.
Load balancing is like distributing dishes among waiters at a restaurant - if one waiter has too many tables, some dishes are reassigned to balance the workload.
The ready queue is like people waiting in line at a ticket counter - they all want service, and the order determines who gets served when.
